hero.subtext: Exclusively curated travel experiences for the Gulf traveler. Explore pristine nature, ancient culture, and absolute comfort.

why.title: Why Ethiopia?
why.ancient_text: Ethiopia is one of the oldest nations on earth, home to Lucy — the 3.2 million-year-old ancestor of all humanity.
why.landscapes_text: From the lowest point in Africa (Danakil -120m) to the highlands of the Simien Mountains at 4,550m, the contrast is staggering.
why.coffee_text: Ethiopia gave the world coffee. The ceremony is still performed three times a day in homes across the country.

destinations.regions_sub: Ethiopia's regions, each a world of its own.

coffee.title: The Coffee Ceremony — Ethiopia's Sacred Ritual
coffee.subtitle: Three rounds. Three hours. One of the world's great cultural experiences.
coffee.step1_text: You are never simply offered coffee in Ethiopia. You are invited into a ceremony. The hostess spreads fresh grass on the floor, burns incense, and arranges the clay jebena pot on charcoal. This is not a drink. This is a welcome.
coffee.step2_text: Raw green coffee beans are washed and roasted over an open flame in a flat pan, stirred constantly. When they smoke and darken, the pan is passed around the room so every guest can inhale the aroma — their first participation in the ceremony.
coffee.step3_text: Coffee is served three times. Abol — the first, strongest round. Tona — the second, slightly lighter. Baraka — the third, meaning 'blessing'. Leaving after only one round is considered rude. Leaving after three is a gift to your host.
coffee.step4_text: Traditional Ethiopian coffee is served with accompaniments — popcorn, peanuts, or kolo (roasted barley). The snacks are not an afterthought. They are part of the ritual, providing something to hold while the conversation unfolds over hours.
coffee.step5_text: The word 'coffee' comes from Kaffa — a region in southwest Ethiopia where wild coffee trees still grow ten metres tall in ancient forest. Every cup of coffee ever drunk anywhere in the world traces its origin to this one forest in this one country.

home.flavors_sub: Ethiopian cuisine is a sensory explosion. Shared from a communal platter, it is a ritual of friendship and family.
home.food_injera_text: The foundation of every meal. A sourdough flatbread made from ancient Teff grain.
home.food_doro_text: A spicy chicken stew simmered for hours with Berbere spice and hard-boiled eggs.
home.food_tibs_text: Sautéed meat chunks with onions, garlic, and peppers. A savory staple of Ethiopian celebration.

home.legitimacy_text: We understand the concerns travelers face when booking tours in Africa. Unlike many unverified operators, Raha Tours is a fully licensed, Ethiopian-owned organization with a proven track record. We don't just sell tours; we provide peace of mind through transparent pricing, verified local partners, and a physical presence in the heart of Addis Ababa. Your safety and satisfaction are not just our goals—they are our legal and moral commitments.
home.legitimacy_item1_text: Operating under strict national tourism regulations with all required permits.
home.legitimacy_item2_text: No hidden fees, no surprises. What we quote is what you pay.
home.legitimacy_item3_text: Owned and operated by Ethiopians who know the land and its people best.

home.testimonial1_quote: Lalibela rewired my brain. I arrived a tourist and left a pilgrim. Raha's team was extraordinary.
home.testimonial2_quote: The Omo Valley experience was unlike anything on earth. Our guide spoke 4 tribal languages — surreal.
home.testimonial3_quote: The coffee ceremony in a local home on Day 1 set the tone for the entire trip. Perfect from start to finish.
home.dest_lalibela_tagline: Where God Carved Churches from Stone
home.khat_home_subtitle: Experience the social heartbeat of Ethiopia through the ancient tradition of Khat.
home.khat_home_text: In the highlands of Ethiopia, the afternoon belongs to Khat. This native plant is more than just a leaf—it is a social glue that brings communities together for social sessions. It is a time of heightened conversation, business deals, and deep cultural connection that travelers find both fascinating and welcoming.
home.ethio_saudi_home_subtitle: A Shared Heritage of Faith, Hospitality, and Brotherhood.
home.ethio_saudi_home_text: From the ancient hospitality provided to early travelers and pilgrims seeking peace to our modern-day cultural exchanges, the bond between Ethiopia and Saudi Arabia is deep and enduring. Experience the warmth of this relationship firsthand in the Land of Origins.

saudi_ethiopia.title: A Shared Heritage
saudi_ethiopia.subtitle: Connecting the Gulf and the Land of Origins through centuries of history, faith, and culture.
saudi_ethiopia.item1_text: Ethiopia (Abyssinia) was the first sanctuary for early pilgrims seeking justice and peace. King Al-Najashi's spirit of protection established a bond of trust that remains strong today.
saudi_ethiopia.item2_text: From the wild forests of Kaffa to the traditional 'Dallah' of the Gulf, our shared love for coffee is more than a drink — it is a symbol of hospitality and connection.
saudi_ethiopia.item3_text: Explore the Negash Mosque, one of the oldest in Africa, and the 99 mosques of Harar, a UNESCO World Heritage city and the 'Fourth Holy City' of Islam.
saudi_ethiopia.item4_text: Discover the deep linguistic and cultural ties reflected in our traditions, architecture, and shared values of family and generosity.

saudi_ethiopia_bonds.title: Bonds of Brotherhood & Love
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.subtitle: Beyond history, it is a relationship of the heart. A shared journey of respect, hospitality, and mutual love between two great nations.
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.community_text: From the bustling streets of Riyadh to the highlands of Addis Ababa, our people share stories, trade, and a profound sense of family that has flourished for generations.
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.hospitality_text: The warmth of a Saudi 'Majlis' finds its mirror in the Ethiopian 'Coffee Ceremony'. Both cultures believe that a guest is a gift, and hospitality is a sacred duty.
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.love_text: It is a bond built on mutual respect and affection. The people of Saudi Arabia and Ethiopia share a unique love for their heritage, their faith, and each other.
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.vision_text: We are building on our glorious past to create a future of shared prosperity, tourism, and cultural exchange that brings our hearts even closer.
saudi_ethiopia_bonds.gallery_subtitle: Moments of friendship and shared history.

book_page.faq1_q: Do I need a visa to visit Ethiopia?
book_page.faq1_a: Most nationalities can obtain an e-visa online at evisa.gov.et within 3 business days. Citizens of some African nations receive a visa on arrival. We provide full visa guidance with every booking.
book_page.faq2_a: The main tourism regions (Addis Ababa, Lalibela, Gondar, Axum, Omo Valley, Simien Mountains) are very safe for tourists. Like any destination, certain regions require awareness — we provide a full pre-trip safety briefing and never route tours through areas of concern.
book_page.faq3_a: Yellow Fever vaccination is required if arriving from a Yellow Fever endemic country. We recommend being up to date on Hepatitis A, Typhoid, and routine vaccinations. Consult your travel health clinic 6-8 weeks before departure.
book_page.faq4_q: What is the best time of year to visit?
book_page.faq4_a: October to February is the peak dry season — ideal for most destinations. November to January is best for cultural festivals including Timkat and Meskel. June to September is the green season — fewer tourists and lusher scenery.
book_page.faq5_q: Can you cater for vegetarians or dietary restrictions?
book_page.faq5_a: Absolutely. Ethiopian cuisine is naturally one of the world's most vegetarian-friendly — fasting foods (yetsom beyaynetu) are widely available and delicious. Inform us of any dietary needs when you book.
book_page.faq6_q: How physically demanding are the tours?
book_page.faq6_a: It varies. The Simien Mountains trek and Danakil Depression tours are physically demanding. The Historic North Circuit and family tours are accessible to most fitness levels. We rate every tour on our difficulty scale and can customize pace.
book_page.faq7_a: Ethiopian Birr is the local currency. We recommend bringing a mix of cash and a Visa/Mastercard for your personal expenses. ATMs are available in Addis Ababa and major towns.
book_page.faq8_q: What language is spoken in Ethiopia?
book_page.faq8_a: Amharic is the official language. English is widely spoken in tourism, hotels, and educated circles. Our guides are fluent in English, and many speak Arabic, French, or other languages on request.
book_page.faq9_q: Do you offer group tours or only private tours?
book_page.faq9_a: Raha specializes in private tours — your guide, your vehicle, your pace. We do not run group tours with strangers. Families and friend groups travel together exclusively.
book_page.faq10_q: What happens if I need to cancel my booking?
book_page.faq10_a: Cancellations more than 60 days before departure receive a full refund. For cancellations closer to departure, we will work with you to reschedule or provide a partial refund based on the specific circumstances. We always aim to be fair and flexible.

dest_page.hero_sub: From the lowest sulfur flats to the highest mountain peaks, Ethiopia's geography is as diverse as its 80+ cultures.
dest_page.deep_dive_history_text: King Lalibela ordered 11 churches carved from a single volcanic rock plateau in the 12th century, intending to create a 'New Jerusalem' in Africa after the fall of the original Jerusalem to Saladin. Construction is said to have involved angels working at night.
dest_page.deep_dive_arch_text: Each church is unique — some are entirely free-standing monoliths, others are carved into cliff faces, and some are connected by subterranean tunnels. Bete Giyorgis (St. George's Church), the most famous, is cruciform in plan and descends 12 metres below ground level.
dest_page.deep_dive_exp_text: Priests in red and gold vestments move between services chanting ancient Ge'ez liturgy. Pilgrims press their foreheads to the stone. The smell of incense, the sound of sistrum rattles, the texture of 900-year-old rock under your hand — Lalibela is fully sensory.
dest_page.deep_dive_getting_text: Daily flights from Addis Ababa (1h 15min). Best visited November to January. Stay 2–3 nights minimum. Raha provides expert Lalibela-born guides who know routes and access points unavailable to independent travelers.
dest_page.seasons_sub: Ethiopia has two main seasons — knowing which suits your itinerary is key.
dest_page.dry_text: Best for trekking, cultural events, wildlife, photography. This is when the highlands are accessible and skies are clear.
dest_page.dry_peak: Peak period: December–February. Book at least 3 months in advance.
dest_page.green_text: Lush landscapes, fewer tourists, lower prices, waterfalls at full power. Ethiopia becomes an emerald paradise during these months.
dest_page.green_peak: The Blue Nile Falls are at their most spectacular in September.
dest_page.festival1_text: The most spectacular festival in the Orthodox calendar, celebrating the baptism of Christ.
dest_page.festival2_text: Commemorates the discovery of the True Cross. Features giant bonfires and yellow flowers.
dest_page.festival3_text: Ethiopian Christmas. Particularly vibrant in Lalibela with thousands of pilgrims in white.
dest_page.festival4_text: The most celebrated festival, following a 55-day fast. A time of great joy and feasting.

entoto.name: ENTOTO MOUNTAIN
entoto.description: Rising 3,200m above Addis Ababa, Entoto is both a historic highland retreat and the spiritual heart of the city.

lalibela.name: LALIBELA ROCK-HEWN CHURCHES
lalibela.description: Carved entirely from living rock in the 12th century, Lalibela's 11 monolithic churches are a feat of human devotion.

semien.name: SIMIEN MOUNTAINS NATIONAL PARK
semien.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Africa's most dramatic highland landscapes.

danakil.name: DANAKIL DEPRESSION
danakil.description: One of the hottest, lowest, and most geologically active places on earth.

lake_tana.name: LAKE TANA & BLUE NILE FALLS
lake_tana.description: Ethiopia's largest lake cradles 37 islands, 21 of which host medieval monasteries.

omo.name: OMO VALLEY TRIBES
omo.description: Home to over 40 distinct ethnic groups, the Omo Valley is one of the last places on earth where ancient cultures survive.

axum.name: AXUM (AKSUM)
axum.description: Once the capital of one of the ancient world's four greatest empires, Axum ruled trade routes from Rome to India.

gondar.name: GONDAR — AFRICA'S CAMELOT
gondar.description: The Camelot of Africa. Gondar's Royal Enclosure contains six medieval castles.

bale.name: BALE MOUNTAINS
bale.description: The Bale Mountains are the stronghold of the Ethiopian wolf — the world's rarest canid.

pack_page.hero_sub: Explore Ethiopia with a tier that fits your style—from modern comfort and electric mobility to the ultimate luxury of private villas and V8 SUVs.
pack_page.itinerary_sub: Day by day breakdown of the Historic North tour.

pack_page.experiences_sub: Every Raha tour is built from these core cultural modules.
pack_page.exp_injera_text: Step into a local home to learn the ancient art of fermenting teff and pouring the perfect injera sourdough.
pack_page.exp_weaving_text: Visit highland villages to see how traditional cotton shammas are woven on handlooms passed down through generations.
pack_page.exp_monastery_text: Exclusive entry into medieval island monasteries on Lake Tana, viewing sacred 14th-century manuscripts.
pack_page.wildlife_sub: Ethiopia is home to species found nowhere else on Earth. Our wildlife safaris are designed to bring you face-to-face with these rare wonders in their natural highland habitats.
pack_page.animal1_text: Found primarily in the Bale Mountains, these elegant red wolves are the crown jewel of Ethiopian wildlife.
pack_page.animal2_text: Endemic to the Simien highlands, these 'grass-monkeys' live in massive, social communities.
pack_page.animal3_text: A majestic goat found only on the sheer cliffs of the Simien Mountains National Park.

pack_page.cta_note: 100% free to enquire. No obligation. We reply within 24 hours.

pack_page.gold_desc: Elevate your experience with premium apartment stays and a modern electric vehicle designed for maximum passenger comfort.
pack_page.plat_desc: The ultimate luxury experience. Enjoy the privacy of a villa and the power of a luxury V8 SUV.
pack_page.plat_check3: Full Arabic and English speaking staff 24/7

stories_page.hero_sub: The moments, the people, the flavors. Written by those who live and breathe the Land of Origins.
stories_page.wisdom_sub: Essential cultural etiquette for your Ethiopian journey.
stories_page.tip1_text: Ethiopians exclusively use the right hand for eating. The left is considered unclean for dining.
stories_page.tip2_text: It's considered polite to accept at least one cup of coffee during a ceremony, even if you're not a coffee drinker.
stories_page.tip3_text: Always ask permission before photographing people, especially in tribal regions where specific etiquette applies.
stories_page.tip4_text: Pointing with a single finger is considered rude. Use your whole hand if you need to gesture.
stories_page.pull_quote: Ethiopia is not a destination you visit and come home from unchanged. It is a place that restructures the furniture in your mind and never quite lets you put it back.
stories_page.hero_subtitle: The moments, the people, the flavors. Written from the road.
stories_page.khat_section_title: The Khat Culture — Ethiopia's Green Leaf
stories_page.khat_section_sub: A social ritual and cultural cornerstone that has defined Ethiopian afternoons for centuries.
stories_page.khat_text_p1: Beyond coffee, Ethiopia is home to Khat (Catha edulis), a flowering plant native to the Horn of Africa. For locals, it is more than just a plant; it is 'Chat'—a catalyst for conversation, a ritual of hospitality, and a pillar of the economy.
stories_page.khat_text_p2: In the walled city of Harar and the highlands of eastern Ethiopia, the afternoons are dedicated to social sessions where friends gather to chew the fresh leaves. It is a time for debate, business, and community connection, often accompanied by tea and incense.
stories_page.khat_tourist_text: Today, many travelers come to Ethiopia to experience this authentic social phenomenon firsthand. Sitting in a local home, sharing stories over fresh leaves, offers a unique window into the social fabric of the nation that traditional sightseeing simply cannot provide.
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_title: The Ethio-Saudi Legacy — A Bond Beyond Time
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_sub: From the Red Sea trade routes to shared horizons, a story of connection, justice, and brotherhood.
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_p1: The relationship between the Land of Origins and the Kingdom is anchored in an ancient spirit of mutual respect. Since the First Hijrah, when Abyssinia provided a safe sanctuary for those seeking justice and peace, Ethiopia has been a land of refuge and friendship. This historical foundation of trust has flourished for over a millennium, bound by the waves of the Red Sea that have carried traders, pilgrims, and scholars between our shores for centuries.
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_p2: Beyond history, our cultures are woven together by the aroma of coffee and shared linguistic roots. Amharic and Arabic share deep Semitic origins, reflected in the names of our towns and the rhythm of our speech. Our shared love for coffee, which traveled from the highland forests of Ethiopia to become a cornerstone of Arabian hospitality, remains a living symbol of the warmth and generosity that defines every home in both our nations.
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_tourist_title: A Welcoming Home for Gulf Travelers
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_tourist_text: Today, for travelers from the Kingdom, Ethiopia offers a familiar sense of family and belonging. From the ancient architecture of the north to the vibrant markets that echo the spirit of the 'Souq', the shared heritage is everywhere. At Raha, we specialize in bridging these two worlds, ensuring every guest experiences the highest honor, warmth, and luxury that our deep-rooted friendship demands.
stories_page.ethio_saudi_bond_image_alt: Detailed representation of the Ethio-Saudi historical connection

story1.title: I Drank Coffee in the Village Where It Was Born
story1.excerpt: A farmer named Girma led us three hours into the Kaffa forest to find wild coffee trees growing ten meters tall. What followed was a communion.
story1.author: Raha Editorial Team

story2.title: The Monks of Lalibela Don't Sleep During Timkat
story2.excerpt: For 48 hours during Timkat, the priests chant and process through subterranean passageways. We were allowed to follow.

story3.title: What It Means to Jump Over a Bull
story3.excerpt: In Hamar culture, a boy becomes a man by running across the backs of 30 bulls. We attended one such ceremony deep in the Omo Valley.

story4.title: Sleeping Beside an Active Volcano
story4.excerpt: We camped on the rim of Erta Ale as molten rock bubbled meters below. At 2am, it exploded. Here is the account of that night.

story5.title: Lucy: The 3.2 Million Year Old Woman
story5.excerpt: She was found in Ethiopia's Afar region in 1974 and proved that humanity began here. We visited the National Museum to see her.

story6.title: The Injera Lesson
story6.excerpt: Injera requires teff, water, and 3 days of fermentation. A grandmother in Addis Ababa invited us into her kitchen to learn.

story7.title: Harar: The City That Feeds Hyenas
story7.excerpt: Every evening at dusk, the Hyena Man calls into the darkness. Within minutes, spotted hyenas emerge to take meat from his lips.

story8.title: Teff: The Ancient Grain
story8.excerpt: Teff is smaller than a poppy seed and has fed highlanders for 3,000 years. We spent a week with a teff farmer to understand its power.

story9.title: Running with the Wind
story9.excerpt: Ethiopia has produced countless distance running champions. We trained for one week with a community club in Bekoji.

story10.title: Gambella: Hidden Wetland Wilderness
story10.excerpt: Deep in the far west lies Gambella, a land of rivers and wetlands. We witnessed the second-largest mammal migration in Africa.

layout.meta_title: Raha Tours | The Land of Origins
layout.meta_desc: Discover the ancient wonders, vibrant cultures, and breathtaking landscapes of Ethiopia with Raha Tours.
